summary.brief: Coleman v. Anniston HMA, LLC. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Virginia Coleman was suffering from gastrointestinal bleeding, that she spent a night in the intensive-care unit of Stringfellow Memorial Hospital operated by Anniston HMA, LLC, d/b/a Stringfellow Memorial Hospital ("the Hospital"), and that she died the next day following surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The nurses had contacted him at 9:40 p.m. regarding Virginia's condition. He ordered tests and ordered that testing recur every two hours; if Virginia's blood levels fell below a certain value, she was to receive a transfusion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Harry Moulis, one of Coleman's experts, opined that additional treatments were available and could have been given to Virginia had the nurses telephoned the doctor.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Harry Moulis

evidence_index.text: Dr. Black testified that he was "fully aware" of the condition that was causing the bleeding and that the records of Virginia's condition on the night in question showed no change that required the nurses to call him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Hospital's second motion for a summary judgment, which the trial court granted.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 27 at 8:30 a.m., Dr. Black examined Virginia. He recommended "Dr. Shaikh scoping her urgently to determine the source of the bleeding."
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An endoscopy was performed on Virginia at 11:27 a.m. A bleeding lesion was found. It was cauterized and injected with a constricting agent, and a clip was applied.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Later that day, Virginia developed respiratory failure, was intubated, and ultimately suffered a full cardiac arrest. She was pronounced dead at 8:07 p.m. on March 27, 2009.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Moulis testified that it was his opinion that there was a delay in treatment of Virginia and that that delay caused her death.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Harry Moulis

evidence_index.text: Henson contended that the Hospital's nursing staff had breached the standard of care because they failed to contact Virginia's physicians during the night of March 26, 2009, and early morning hours of March 27.
evidence_index.speaker: Lisa Henson

evidence_index.text: Viewing the evidence in a light most favorable to Coleman, the nonmovant, and entertaining such reasonable inferences as a jury would have been free to draw, as we are required to do under our summary-judgment standard of review, I conclude that there is no disconnect between Henson's testimony and Dr. Moulis's testimony.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Coleman has presented substantial evidence that the Hospital breached the applicable standard of care in its treatment of Virginia.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Dr. Black's assertion that he would not have changed his course of treatment even if he had been told that Virginia's condition was worsening does not conclusively establish that the nurses' care and treatment of Virginia during the overnight hours in no way caused or contributed to her death.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Vomiting blood, headaches, and abdominal pain
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Virginia Coleman presented to the emergency department with a history of vomiting blood, headaches, and abdominal pain. She had a past medical history of a bleeding ulcer and three cardiac stents and was on anticoagulants.

requestables.name: Complete Blood Count
requestables.description: To assess hemoglobin levels and overall blood loss

requestables.name: Endoscopy
requestables.description: To identify the source of gastrointestinal bleeding

reveal.result_summary: Bleeding lesion found, cauterized, and treated

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

timeline_phases.name: ICU Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Patient monitored in ICU with ongoing GI bleed

timeline_phases.name: Morning Assessment
timeline_phases.description: Physician assesses patient in the morning

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ICU with ongoing gastrointestinal bleeding
decision_points.prompt: Should the nursing staff contact the physician again during the night?

options.description: Contact the physician to report worsening condition
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires notifying physician of significant changes

options.description: Do not contact the physician, continue monitoring
options.clinical_reasoning: Nurses believed no change required notification

actual_action_defendant.description: Nurses did not contact the physician during the night

expected_action_court.description: Nurses should have contacted the physician
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated need for physician notification

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to notify physician of worsening condition
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Nurses should have escalated care by contacting the physician
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed respiratory failure and cardiac arrest, pronounced dead

key_findings: Dr. Black's testimony indicated no change in treatment would have occurred
Summary judgment granted in favor of the Hospital

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Notification might not have changed outcome

allegations.allegation: Failure to notify physician of worsening condition

court_findings.finding: No breach of duty by nursing staff
court_findings.legal_significance: Summary judgment in favor of Hospital
